justification: All pass criteria met: CAP theorem tension explicitly identified in executive_summary and escalations. Escalation clearly asks product team to choose between consistency and availability with three concrete options. Design explicitly states consistency is prioritized and availability is sacrificed during failover (CP system). Team size constraint deeply factored into every decision (monolith over microservices, PostgreSQL over distributed locks, rejected etcd/ZooKeeper). trade_offs section leads with the CAP trade-off as the first entry with clear gains/sacrifices/alternatives.

observations.text: CAP theorem tension identified immediately in executive_summary and thoroughly explained throughout
Escalation is well-structured with three concrete options (CP, AP, Hybrid) and a clear recommendation
Design explicitly states which property is sacrificed: availability during 10-30s failover window
Team size constraint drives multiple architectural decisions: monolith, single DB, no Redis/etcd/ZooKeeper
SKIP LOCKED pattern is a genuinely strong technical recommendation for this problem
Confidence level appropriately set at 60% with clear low-confidence areas around unresolved product decisions

agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"The courier assignment system has a fundamental CAP theorem tension: requirement #2 (strong consistency — no double-assignment) directly conflicts with requirement #3 (availability during database node failure). These cannot be simultaneously guaranteed during a network partition. The recommended architecture prioritizes consistency (CP) for the assignment operation itself — a double-assigned courier is a real-world safety/SLA violation — while maximizing availability throu
